messages.content: You are a helpful AI assistant which rephrase the following text in your own words.
I'd be happy to help you rephrase the text! Please provide me with the text you would like me to rephrase.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: While this microphone might seem mostly the same as my previous favorite, the Samson Q2U, I now recommend the Q2U only if you're on a really tight budget—the Q2U has been as low as $35! But I think the ATR2100x-USB (specifically the newer “x” model) is worth the extra cost because of several seemingly small but greatly appreciated improvements:

Although the Samson Q2U microphone may appear similar to my previous favorite, there are some notable differences. While the Q2U is suitable for those with a limited budget, with its lowest price reaching $35, I believe that the ATR2100x-USB, particularly the newer "x" model, justifies the additional expense due to various minor enhancements that are highly valued.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: The main complication you might run into when recording over USB is that the mic does not loop back USB audio. So your guest's audio will not be recorded with your ATR2100x-USB unless you add a little only-moderately-complicated input-mixing on your computer.

One potential issue you may encounter when recording via USB is that the microphone does not have the ability to loop back USB audio. As a result, if you are using the ATR2100x-USB, you will not be able to record your guest's audio unless you implement a moderately complex input-mixing setup on your computer.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: The MV7 works best when paired with the ShurePlus MOTIV app so you can control the microphone's built-in EQ, limiter, and compressor. You can also use the app to adjust how the headphone monitoring is mixed between how much of the mic and how much of your device audio you hear (if you have the MV7 connected to something via USB for additional audio).

For optimal performance, it is recommended to use the ShurePlus MOTIV app alongside the MV7 microphone. This allows you to have control over the microphone's built-in EQ, limiter, and compressor settings. Additionally, the app enables you to adjust the mix of headphone monitoring, allowing you to determine the balance between the microphone's audio and the audio from your connected device (if you have the MV7 connected to another device via USB for additional audio).

Rephrase the following text in your own words: The one feature I dislike about the MV7 compared to the ATR2100x-USB is that the MV7's headphone monitoring is subject to your USB-connected device's output volume. So if you're connected to a PC and you turn down the PC's volume, you'll hear your own voice less through the MV7's headphones output. But the ATR2100x-USB maintains your self-monitoring level no matter the input or output volumes of the USB-connected device.

One aspect of the MV7 that I find unfavorable when comparing it to the ATR2100x-USB is its headphone monitoring, as it is dependent on the output volume of the USB-connected device. Therefore, if you are connected to a PC and lower the volume on the computer, the sound of your own voice through the MV7's headphone output will also decrease. In contrast, the ATR2100x-USB maintains a consistent self-monitoring level regardless of the input or output volumes of the connected USB device.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: Like the ATR2100x-USB, the main complication you might run into when recording over USB is that the mic does not loop back USB audio. So your guest's audio will not be recorded with your MV7 unless you add a little only-moderately-complicated input-mixing on your computer.

Similar to the ATR2100x-USB, one potential challenge you may encounter when recording via USB with the MV7 is the lack of USB audio loopback functionality. Consequently, when using the MV7, your guest's audio will not be recorded unless you implement a moderately complex input-mixing setup on your computer.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: I love the quality, sound, and physical design of the Shure SM7B. I love how it has built-in shock-protection and its XLR plug is vertical instead of horizontal, so it helps with cable management and concealment. The main thing I don't like about the SM7B is how much gain it needs in order to avoid background hiss in your recordings. You need a really good preamp for the SM7B, so that means either additional hardware or upgrading your gear.

I am enthusiastic about the outstanding quality, sound, and physical attributes of the Shure SM7B microphone. Its built-in shock-protection and vertical XLR plug contribute to better cable management and concealment, which I appreciate. However, one drawback of the SM7B is its high gain requirement to prevent background hiss in recordings. To effectively use the SM7B, a high-quality preamp is necessary, which implies the need for additional hardware or gear upgrade.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: The latest generations of interfaces and recorders—like the Focusrite Vocaster, Zoom Podtrack P4, and RØDE RØDECaster Pro II—do finally provide enough gain without needing an additional preamp. But if you're on anything older, you might have to deal with background hiss until you upgrade.

Newer models of interfaces and recorders, such as the Focusrite Vocaster, Zoom Podtrack P4, and RØDE RØDECaster Pro II, have addressed the issue of insufficient gain by offering enough amplification without requiring an additional preamp. However, if you are using older equipment, you may still encounter background hiss until you upgrade to these newer models.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: I usually recommend what are generally called “studio microphones” because the best place to record a microphone is usually in some kind of “studio” setting—whether that's a closet in your basement or a fancy recording studio. Just like you can be the “CEO of your cubicle,” any dedicated place you record your podcast can be your “studio.”

Typically, I suggest utilizing what are commonly referred to as "studio microphones" as they are designed for optimal recording in a studio environment. This setting can range from a simple closet in your basement to a professional recording studio. Similar to how you can excel and take charge of your work even in a cubicle, any dedicated space where you record your podcast can be considered your personal "studio."

Rephrase the following text in your own words: Many microphones serve the unconventional needs of being away from a studio. For example, a handheld microphone for “man on the street” interviews, a micro headset mic for active movement or public presentations, a mic and headphones combo headset for noisy environments (like at a sports game), a wireless mic for videos or crowded spaces, a hidden or unobtrusive mic for natural-looking videos, and more! Your unique needs might be better served by something that is not a studio dynamic microphone!

Numerous microphones cater to non-traditional requirements where being outside of a studio setting is necessary. For instance, handheld microphones are suitable for conducting "man on the street" interviews, while micro headset mics are ideal for active movement or public presentations. A combination of mic and headphones in a headset form is beneficial in noisy environments, such as sports games. Wireless mics are ideal for videos or crowded spaces, and hidden or unobtrusive mics are advantageous for creating natural-looking videos. It's important to consider that your specific needs might be better fulfilled by a microphone that is not specifically designed for studio use, as there are various options available tailored to different circumstances.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: I like this microphone because, in my experience, it's the most natural-sounding and complimentary for all voices. Plus, its strong neodymium magnets make this mic need less gain than most other dynamic mics popular for podcasters, so the RE320 is much friendlier for whatever recorder or interface you're using.

In my personal experience, I appreciate this microphone due to its ability to capture natural and well-balanced sound for all types of voices. Additionally, the RE320 microphone utilizes powerful neodymium magnets, which results in requiring less gain compared to other dynamic microphones commonly used by podcasters. This characteristic makes the RE320 microphone compatible with a wide range of recorders or interfaces, enhancing its overall versatility and compatibility.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: I originally bought into the hype of the Heil PR40 when I bought my first good microphone. And it is indeed a good microphone! But I started hearing it too much in all the podcasts I listened to, and I started noticing all those podcasts emphasized the same frequencies that sounded more “radio booming” instead of natural and relatable. And the more I heard my own voice on the PR40 (even though I didn't use the same multiband compression settings many other PR40 podcasters used), I was regularly annoyed with some audio artifacts. So when I heard Dave Jackson, from School of Podcasting, switch to a RE320 and his voice suddenly sounded so much clearer and more natural, I knew I wanted that microphone!

When I initially purchased a high-quality microphone, I fell for the popularity surrounding the Heil PR40, and it proved to be a good microphone indeed. However, as I listened to podcasts, I noticed that the PR40 was commonly used, resulting in a similar emphasis on specific frequencies, creating a more "radio booming" sound rather than a natural and relatable one. Moreover, despite not using the same multiband compression settings as other PR40 users, I still encountered audio artifacts in my own voice when using the PR40, which became a regular annoyance. However, when I heard Dave Jackson, from the School of Podcasting, switch to the RE320 microphone, his voice became noticeably clearer and more natural, leading me to desire that microphone for myself.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: Electro-Voice was so kind to send me the RE320 (review), RE20 (review), and RE27N/D for testing and review (but I couldn't keep them). Even though I had more highly praised the RE20 in my original reviews, the more I watched my own videos and listened to myself on the RE320, the more I loved its natural sound. Then, I had the opportunity to meet and interview Rick Belt, the creator of the RE320, at NAB Show 2015. He had already loved the reviews I had made, and when I told him that I was “coveting” the RE320 as I was using my PR40, and I planned to someday switch, he pulled one from a cabinet and gave it to me! So in full disclosure, I have not paid for the RE320 I use, but I would have switched anyway when I could afford it and I wish I had always used the RE320 instead of the Heil PR40!

Electro-Voice generously provided me with the RE320, RE20, and RE27N/D microphones for testing and review purposes (although I couldn't keep them). Initially, in my original reviews, I had praised the RE20 highly. However, as I continued to watch my own videos and listen to my recordings on the RE320, I developed a deep appreciation for its natural sound. At the NAB Show 2015, I had the privilege of meeting and interviewing Rick Belt, the creator of the RE320. He was already aware of the positive reviews I had given and when I expressed my strong desire to switch to the RE320 from my PR40, he graciously handed me one from a cabinet. So, to be completely transparent, I did not purchase the RE320 I currently use, but even if I had to buy it, I would have gladly made the switch as soon as I could afford it. Looking back, I truly wish I had always used the RE320 instead of the Heil PR40.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: If you're using a Blue Yeti or a similar microphone that's cumbersome, difficult to work with, or requires you to balance like a TV antenna from the old days just to get some good audio, you should probably upgrade. I remember that it was such a wonderful upgrade for me when I bought my first lav mic for recording videos because I didn't have to have a big studio mic in the shot or frame my shots close to my face! And then when I upgraded to a wireless system, I saved a lot of time and frustration from trying to align my separately recorded audio and video!

If you find yourself using a Blue Yeti or a similar microphone that proves to be inconvenient, challenging to handle, or forces you to position yourself like an old-fashioned TV antenna just to achieve decent audio quality, it may be time to consider an upgrade. I distinctly recall the joy I experienced when I purchased my first lav mic for recording videos, as it eliminated the need for a large studio microphone to be in the frame or to position my shots close to my face. Furthermore, upgrading to a wireless system provided immense time and relief by eliminating the hassle of aligning separately recorded audio and video.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: If you're already using one of the specific mics I recommend and it's meeting your needs, then you have to consider whether the maybe 5–10% boost in quality is worth spending an additional $300—or more if you need additional connecting gear! In other words, your podcast might have 99 problems, but is your microphone really one of them? You might do better to invest that money elsewhere to fix your audio issues or grow your podcast.

If you are currently using one of the recommended microphones that I suggest and it is satisfying your requirements, it is important to assess whether the potential 5-10% increase in quality justifies spending an extra $300 or possibly more for additional connecting equipment. In simpler terms, you should consider if your microphone is truly a significant problem among the many challenges your podcast might be facing. It might be more beneficial to allocate that money towards other areas to address audio issues or to support the growth of your podcast.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: I recommend Audacity exactly because it's free and yet still very capable! Unlike GarageBand (which is free on macOS), Audacity sticks to conventional audio-editing tools and principles. These conventions make it easier to learn audio-editing on Audacity and later move up to more advanced software. Plus, the latest versions of Audacity are making huge leaps forward in improving the software.

One of the reasons I recommend Audacity is because it is a free software that offers impressive capabilities. In contrast to GarageBand, which is free for macOS users, Audacity adheres to traditional audio-editing tools and principles. This adherence to industry conventions simplifies the learning process for audio editing on Audacity, enabling users to transition more seamlessly to advanced software in the future. Additionally, the latest versions of Audacity showcase significant advancements and improvements that further enhance the functionality of the software.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: Several years ago, I forced myself to step up from Audacity to Adobe Audition, and I found it significantly improved my workflow. There were several things that Audition made faster or easier for me, and yet there were some things Audition couldn't do that Audacity did. However, many of those items might be moot at this point.

A few years back, I made the decision to transition from Audacity to Adobe Audition, and I experienced a notable enhancement in my workflow. Adobe Audition provided several features and capabilities that allowed me to work more efficiently and effortlessly. However, it is worth mentioning that there were certain tasks that Audacity could perform but were not possible in Audition. Nevertheless, it is possible that some of those limitations have become irrelevant by now.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: Audition is premium software that comes at a price. And unfortunately, that is only a subscription price: currently $31.49 per month without a contract, or $239.88 per year (essentially $19.99 per month) at the “best” price. (There's tax on these prices, too.)

Audition is a high-quality software that comes with a price tag, and regrettably, it is only available through a subscription model. Presently, the monthly subscription costs $31.49 without a contract, or you can opt for the yearly subscription at a cost of $239.88, which equates to approximately $19.99 per month. It is worth noting that these prices also include applicable taxes.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: However, if you are a slave to Adobe Creative Cloud, like I am, then you already have Adobe Audition along with all of Adobe's other software in the complete subscription suite for $599 per year (about $50 per month, plus tax). I already pay for Adobe Audition along with Photoshop, Illustrator, InDesign, Adobe Fonts, Premiere Pro, Lightroom, and After Effects. And it's because I'm already paying for it, that I use Adobe Audition with my audio-producer John Bukenas. But if I didn't need the rest of Adobe's apps, I would switch to my #1 podcast-editing app in a heartbeat.

However, if you are an avid user of Adobe Creative Cloud, much like myself, you may already have access to Adobe Audition bundled with the complete subscription suite, which costs $599 per year (approximately $50 per month, plus tax). Since I already pay for Adobe Audition as part of my subscription, which also includes other software like Photoshop, Illustrator, InDesign, Adobe Fonts, Premiere Pro, Lightroom, and After Effects, I utilize Adobe Audition in collaboration with my audio producer John Bukenas. However, if I didn't require the other applications offered by Adobe, I would swiftly switch to my preferred podcast-editing application.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: At $239.88 (plus tax) per year, I think Adobe Audition is too expensive for most podcasters unless they already subscribe to Adobe Creative Cloud. So if you can afford it, then I think Audition is one of the best audio-editing apps you can use!

In my opinion, at a cost of $239.88 per year (plus tax), Adobe Audition may be considered too pricey for the majority of podcasters, unless they are already subscribed to Adobe Creative Cloud. If you have the financial means to afford it, I believe that Audition is among the top-notch audio-editing applications available for use.
